title: A menu on two levels
statement:
An empty editor. Build the menu you see in the target on the right.

Eight things. Two of them are groups, the other six belong under them — not alongside them.

examples.explain: The sublist is INSIDE the item it belongs to. Not next to it.

checks.t7: The things are in the sublists

why.empty: The editor is empty. There is no starter code here — you build the list yourself.
why.not-closed: A tag is open and never closed. A nested list has two ends, and both are on the inside.
why.no-heading: There is no heading, or it is empty. The menu wants a name.
why.few-items: There are not eight things. Two groups and six under them make eight.
why.empty-items: There is a list item with no text in it. An empty box counts for nothing.
why.no-sublists: There are no sublists. Everything sits on one level, as if all eight things were equal. They are not.
why.not-nested: You made a sublist, but it is NEXT TO the group, not INSIDE it. On screen it looks the same — indented, with bullets. But to the browser these are two separate lists side by side. The water is not under drinks — it is beside them.
why.too-deep: You nested a third level. There are two levels here: groups and the things under them. Nothing goes under something that is already under something.

not-nested: Look at where you close the of the group. Close it before the sublist and the sublist stays outside.

A similar case:
  • Fruit
    • Apple
    — here the apple is NOT under the fruit. The right way is
  • Fruit
    • Apple
    • .
The group opens, then the whole sublist comes, and only then the group closes. A box inside a box.
no-sublists: The two groups are not things to eat. They are names of categories.
Each group wants its own list underneath it.
few-items: Count: two groups, three things under each.
The groups are list items too. They count.

solution.why:
The first instinct is to write the eight things one under another and call it a list. It looks like a menu — but it is not. Eight equal things, where the water stands level with drinks.

The second instinct is to put the sublist right after the group, separately. On screen it looks exactly right: indented, with bullets. But those are two separate lists standing side by side. The browser does not know the water belongs under drinks.
steps: Open the outer list. It will hold only two things — the groups.
Open the <li> of the first group, but do NOT close it.
Inside that open <li>, put a whole <ul> with the three things from that group.
Only after the closing do you close the of the group.
solution.note: Nesting is not indentation. Indentation is something you see. Nesting is something the code says. Two things can look identical and mean something completely different.
